title: The Second Sunday after Christmas
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
Sermon Date: January 3, 2021

## 1. LITURGICAL CONTEXT

The Second Sunday after Christmas falls within the Christmas Season, which extends from December 25 through the Feast of the Epiphany (January 6). In the Revised Common Lectionary, this Sunday typically features Jeremiah 31:7-14 or Sirach 24:1-12 as the Old Testament reading, with Psalm 147:12-20, Ephesians 1:3-14, and John 1:1-18 (the Prologue) as the Gospel. The readings emphasize the Word made flesh and God's gathering of the dispersed, making this a thematically rich moment between the nativity and Epiphany, when churches reflect on the incarnation's cosmic significance.

## 2. EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church was in the period following General Convention 2018 (held triennially), with implementation of resolutions still underway. In the Diocese of Colorado, Bishop Kimberly Lucas had been serving since 2015 and was actively engaged in diocesan reconciliation work. Early 2021 marked continued navigation of pandemic-related worship restrictions that had been in place since March 2020, with the church facing decisions about in-person worship capacity and safety protocols as vaccination campaigns were just beginning nationwide.

## 3. DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver was experiencing winter weather typical for early January, with the region having seen significant snow in late December. Downtown Denver and the Capitol Hill neighborhood where St. John's Cathedral is located were quiet following the New Year holiday, with businesses reopening after January 1. Colorado remained under state public health orders related to COVID-19, affecting church gathering sizes and protocols. The aftermath of the January 6 Capitol riot (which had not yet occurred as of January 3) was not yet a factor, but the pre-riot political tensions were heightened in Colorado as elsewhere.

## 4. NATIONAL NEWS (US)

The country was in the immediate aftermath of the 2020 presidential election and contested transition period, with the Electoral College vote certification scheduled for January 6-7 (three days after this sermon). COVID-19 hospitalizations were at record levels, with the nation averaging over 3,000 daily deaths as the Delta wave built. The Pfizer and Moderna vaccines had begun rolling out in mid-December with very limited supply, creating national anxiety about access. Georgia was holding runoff elections on January 5 for two U.S. Senate seats that would determine control of the chamber, with intense national focus and campaign activity.

## 5. INTERNATIONAL NEWS

The UK had just implemented strict lockdown measures on January 4 as the Omicron variant drove case surges, making this a moment of tightening restrictions across the Atlantic. India was beginning its vaccination campaign with AstraZeneca vaccines, facing logistical challenges across its vast population. The ongoing Syrian civil war and Yemen humanitarian crisis continued with limited international attention, as COVID-19 dominated global headlines.

## 6. CULTURAL MOMENT

The national mood was one of exhaustion, anxiety, and deep political division, now entering a year (2021) that many hoped would bring relief but few expected would be simple. Holiday gatherings had proceeded despite warnings, with many Americans uncertain whether they had been exposed to COVID-19. The cultural conversation was dominated by pandemic fatigue, election anxiety, and vaccination uncertainty. In Denver specifically, as a relatively progressive city in a swing state, residents were acutely aware of the Senate runoff significance and the January 6 certification date looming just days away. Sports remained a steady cultural anchor, with the NFL playoffs beginning the following week.
